content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 4, 2015

Dawn Haggerty
30717 Emperor Drive
Canyon Lake, CA 92587

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-018(a)

Dear Ms. Haggerty:

This letter supplements our recent advice letter to you dated February 20, 2015, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ To reiterate, our advice is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

In our previous letter, we examined whether you were subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions by virtue of your position as a member of the Public Safety Ad Hoc Committee (the "Committee"). We concluded that because the Committee did not have decisionmaking authority, you were not a "public official" subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions in your capacity as a Committee member.

Despite this conclusion, we recognize that you are still subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions in your capacity as a councilmember for the City of Canyon Lake. And because you stated that you would like to report the findings of the Committee to the City Council, we have decided to supplement our previous letter in order to briefly discuss whether such communications could be construed as an attempt to improperly influence a governmental decision under the Act.

As you know, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her interests, such as real property, as specified by Section 87103. In particular, for a decision before the official's

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-018a
Page No. 2


own agency, a public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member,
officer, employee, or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) Therefore, in your
situation, by communicating with the City Council about findings of the Committee, you
potentially could be influencing a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on one or more of your financial interests.

However, even if a public official determines that his or her interests will experience a
material financial effect as a result of the governmental decision before the official, he or she
may still participate under the "public generally" exception if the material financial effect of a
governmental decision on the public official's interests is indistinguishable from its effect on
the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

Under the basic public generally rule, an official must meet a two-part test by showing
that the decision would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantially the same
manner" as it financially affects the official's interest. Assuming you own a home in Canyon
Lake, the "significant segment" would be defined as 10-percent or more of all property owners
or all residential property owners in Canyon Lake, or 5,000 property owners or residential
property owners in the City.

Your facts state that 11,000 of the 12,500 Canyon Lake residents live within the same
gated community that is governed by a homeowner's association. Canyon Lake will consider
using the law enforcement services of a City Police Department instead of the current Sheriff's
Department. In this case, it is plain that such a conversion will affect a "significant segment" of
Canyon Lake's residents in "substantially the same manner."

Assuming your interests would experience a material financial affect as a result of the
change in the provision of law enforcement services, the "public generally" exception would
nevertheless apply to allow you to make, participate in making, or use your official position to
influence that governmental decision. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit you from
communicating the findings of the Committee to the City Council.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

By:  Jack Woodside
     Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City councilmember can communicate findings of a Public Safety Ad Hoc Committee to the City Council when such communication could influence a governmental decision regarding law enforcement services that would materially affect the councilmember's financial interests as a property owner.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the councilmember may communicate the Committee findings to the City Council because the 'public generally' exception applies—the decision to switch law enforcement services would affect a significant segment (11,000 of 12,500 residents) of Canyon Lake residents in substantially the same manner as it affects the councilmember's interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Supplemental advice letter (references prior February 20, 2015 letter). No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION headers, but question and conclusion are clearly articulated in body text. Strong confidence in extraction due to clear legal analysis structure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a City councilmember can communicate findings of a Public Safety Ad Hoc Committee to the City Council when such communication could influence a governmental decision regarding law enforcement services that would materially affect the councilmember's financial interests as a property owner.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that the councilmember may communicate the Committee findings to the City Council because the 'public generally' exception applies—the decision to switch law enforcement services would affect a significant segment (11,000 of 12,500 residents) of Canyon Lake residents in substantially the same manner as it affects the councilmember's interests.

embedding.summary: This supplemental advice letter addresses whether a City councilmember subject to conflict of interest provisions can report findings to the City Council regarding law enforcement services. The FPPC concludes the councilmember may do so under the 'public generally' exception since the proposed change would affect the vast majority of residents.
